But I think having a self-serve tool out and also trying to do this at the same time, it's like wearing a weighted vest.
You kind of have to keep this.
Like, my tool is amazing.
It's self-serve.
You can come and you get all the results that you want, but it's, like, kind of a lie.
And then at the same time, you're trying to deliver the service but maintain the self-serve tool.
Like, I think it's just, like, an art or go-to-market.
And then for us, it's, like, we just abstract that all away.
You just pay us some very high, like, large amount of money, you know, and then we'll just figure it out.
But, like, you don't care if it takes us 10 minutes or 10 hours to do this task.
You just care that we, like, we do it, you know?
Yeah.
So I kind of like that.
Like, I feel like that's, like, a more likely way to get to, like, this, I don't know, whatever the future of, like, products is, I feel like this will probably get there faster.
Like, I feel like in the future, people won't buy, like, self-serve SaaS products.
They'll just buy, like, an outcome or, like, a person.
And then whether that person's human or not, like, you don't care.
You know, when I hire a lawyer, I just care if they do a good job, not that, you know, I'm actually hired.
I think I was bought into that.
Buy an outcome.
I like circled that.
I think it's already happening.
When you guys did the BPO, you're buying an outcome of that agent.
Exactly.
Who you can buy is just more and more sophisticated, I guess.
At some point, you can buy a lawyer.
You can't right now.
I mean, you kind of do it already.
There's the legaldocs.com sort of thing where you buy what's in a lawyer's brain for an NDA or something like that.
Oh, okay.
I've never seen that one.
I know Harvey is the other example.

I think for Harvey, you're buying some paralegal domain expert, and then you just ask questions like, oh, you're buying an M&A corporate law expert, and then you can ask them questions, and they make it really nice, I think.
Yeah.
I mean, that's mainly why I joined.
Also, was just a good time, think.
It feels really bad to, like, try to grow a product that hasn't figured out, like, product market fit, you know?
And I know Colby's kind of going through that now, trying to figure out how to sell a product that hasn't hit product market fit.
I think for me, it's just the timing to do this seemed right, you know, when I was bought in.
Yeah.
Well, yeah, I was really excited to talk to you because Bridget was, like, Jason, like, came in here and was like, I want to know how it works and get my hands dirty.
And that's totally the way that I learn and, like, want to jump into things, too.
So it's been interesting to hear your perspective.
And I am happy to meet people that, like, want to, like, be in the problem and know what, like, gets the heart of it.
So.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, that's great to hear.
I think that's what you need to be.
From now on, like, unless you're like, I feel like there's this hump, like, maybe you're like a senior CMO type person, I guess you don't have to worry.
But like, if you're not, and you're just managing people, I feel like, I don't know, I feel like that goes away at some point.
Like, yeah, you need to be able to do the work and manage the AIs.
You know, you can't do that if you don't know how to do the work.
Totally.
Okay, cool.
I mean, is there anything, I guess on your end, like, how are you making this decision?
Is there anything else I could tell you to how kind of sway you in our direction?
Because I think, I mean, it seems like you probably are the right person.
I'm trying to think like, what else I could ask to.
Like, maybe, like, just, I guess, do you have a good sense of, like, where the problems are, like, in the domain that you would, you know, sit, like, if you did join?
Maybe I can share some more there.
Well, yeah, I mean, I think it's a question of priority, right?
Right, so.

Like I said, Bridget has this idea of the problem being that internal feedback loop, but then also thinking about the problem in terms of standardizing deliverables even before the product and platform that you're building internally are ready.
You know, those are both things that I would and have worked on at Tango.
Like I built our, our like launch process at Tango.
So the team had an idea of what's coming.
And so, but that's something else that I'm good at is getting alignment on like, what is the biggest problem?
And then working with people to help solve it.
Often because like you're talking to the client, you can't take time out to think about holistically about the problem.
And so I think like something that I did a lot at Tango was come in as a third, like a little bit more.
I had like that step back and I could help revamp the process so that it worked for the next three people that you hire.

And then, you know, you might need to revamp it again when you get to 10 people or 15 people or whatever it is, but prioritizing those problems and then helping solve them with a bit more of an objective lens.
Yeah, I think that's the right mindset.
mean, I don't know, like I would hate to just like implement a bunch of tools without thinking that through what we're actually trying to accomplish.
mean, to a very good point, I think that like tactically, that is like an important thing to do.
But like, at a high level, I think we're sort of right now, like the areas for us is like, I guess, like getting a new customer.
And then there's kind of this three month period at the beginning where a lot of them are in the pilot where we kind of have to like show results.
Yeah.
Bridget was saying, she's like, we got to end the pilot.
Yeah, we probably should end the pilots, but I mean.
It's like signing on a year at a fairly expensive thing.
It's understandable on a pilot.
And even if they don't get a pilot, I'm sure they'll find some way of not paying us if we're not delivering.
So there's kind of that pilot period and then upsells and expansion is there.
I think by far the main thing for us is just the ability to deliver in that pilot period and beyond.
But mostly like if you figure out in the pilot period, think there's like good cadence there.
Yeah.
And there's a lot of things that factor into like our ability to deliver everything from like it's kind of weird.
Like sometimes when we can't deliver, like you can solve it with the product that we're building.

So today I spent a bunch of time this morning just talking about the like client projects that are kind of at risk because the way we built our workflows and the way our kind of pods work, like we're just having a lot of trouble meeting that expectation.

You know, because, like, our AI generation can't generate the right type of content or the kind of writers are, like, they just don't know how to use it properly, you know, like, from all sorts of problems.
Yeah, so I think solving that is, like, by far the most important thing.
And I guess I feel like it has to be a collaboration.
So, like, it would be, like, me, because I guess for now I'm, like, the voice of, like, the pods on the team.
Yeah, it sounds like it.
Like, I have, like, intentionally I only have, you know, five or six clients, which still sounds like a lot.
But then some of the other directors have, like, 12.
Just so I have time to, like, think about these problems, you know, with somebody like you and with Daniel.
Yeah, so, like, product is, like, a solution.
And then I think there's just so much process.
Like, like, the customer feels is really the experience that, like, whoever this person is bringing, whether it's you or somebody else is going to.
How do we send messages and what messages do we try to push our team to send?
What are the rituals, I guess, that we do weekly?
To your point, you guys do a monthly review and a weekly something else, some sort of document.
How do you come up with those things?
If we're going to build a portal, what's in there?
What's the most important thing for them to see?
What's the most important action we want them to take?
It's just like designing this weird agency that's actually a product experience for them.
Yeah.
Yeah, is where my head would be at.
And then upselling, I feel like it's just not as important.
We need to actually deliver so people don't churn.
Yeah.
And getting more customers.
Marcel is like just a magical sales machine.
Like you just keep bringing in more customers.
So I don't think we have to worry there yet.
Yeah.
Cool.
All right.
Well.
I mean, it seems like there's really exciting and interesting problems to solve, so.
Okay, cool.
I don't know what process Bridget had in mind, but I'll talk to her right after and then let me know if you have any questions.
Yeah, and then we can go from there.
Cool.
Thanks, Jason.
Alrighty.
Thanks, Andy.
Talk to you later.
Have a good one.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
